sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah, we jumped down.
Nobody was in the corner.
Nobody was, was standing.
So we jumped.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
We, we go on the-- on the main road-- main road to Kobrin.
And he say he have some relatives in Kobrin, and he's going there.
I told him, it's the same would be in Kobrin because [LAUGHS] it's a ghetto there, and there's no use to go.
And now he's going.
He's going there.
And I stay.
And I-- and I have the, the, the farms what I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] run was-- one was close to the-- to the highway.
So I went there.
But I, I don't-- I don't find anybody.
And I go back.
And I, I know I have to go somewhere in the woods to, to-- looking for partisans because we, we hear before is that many run from the army, from the Russian Army.
Many not run, but stay, stay, stay because they didn't manage to, to run away.
So-- and they organized the partisans.
So I have to go looking for them.
And I went-- [SIGHS] and I went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a, a, a town near [NON-ENGLISH].
And, and they-- it was-- it was-- it was dark.
And I came to a-- to a-- to a country village, a small country village close to the canal.
And I was afraid for people because nobody never knows whom you, you can meet.
And-- but I meet some nice Belorussian people.
And I, I told them when I'm going.
And, and they are very-- they were very pro-- they were against, against-- they were against the German.
And they told me when you cut across the canal.
On the other side, there are big, big woods.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And if you're lucky, you'd find some partisan Russian soldiers or-- and they told me where to go through the canal in the-- in the-- in the place it's not so deep.
But still, it was-- it was deep, like, [POINTS] over the-- and I went through.
And I went-- and I come to the woods.
And I lay overnight under a tree.
And in the morning, when I-- when I go up, I was looking where to go.
And I saw a, a, a boy-- a boy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] looking, looking after, after some cows on the-- on the-- on the-- on the-- on the grass.
So I went to him.
Because one boy, I was not afraid for him.
I go him.
And I ask him how-- did he see some people up here.
Oh, yes, he, he saw some people.
And he showed me where to go.
And I found some people.
But he, he, he, he knows I am Jewish.
And, and he know where the Jews who runs away, some.
And he told me where to go in, in the direction.
And I went.
And I went maybe an hour or something.
And I-- and I meet a group-- a group, Jewish--
--Jewish people.
They, they were from the-- from the ghetto, from the other places.
I didn't ask from where they are, but it was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bad conditions.
Some are burnt, and black faces, and their hands black.
It was a terrible-- a terrible look and terrible situation.
And it was very bad conditions.
So I have some-- from the peasant, I saved some bread.
And I gave some of the bread.
And, and [SIGHS] we spent some time.
And, and they know-- they hear about-- too about the partisans, what for to do.
They have to find some people who, [CRYING] who can protect us.
[CRYING] So nobody-- not, not everybody could walk and, and, and go.
So we--
--it was--
About 15 or something, more or less, yes.
No, I saw these burns.
But what can they tell?
We saw what, what happened, so we didn't ask what happened.
We saw them.
So-- and it was one, one boy, and another boy, a young man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reznik, from the-- from the village.
And, and he said, come with me.
We, we find some-- so we went.
We leave them.
And we went.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we was walking.
And we find some-- many shelters from Jewish.
There was three person, four person.
They make some, some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tents, like, like, tents from, from, from, from branches, from the wood.
And they were sitting.
And, and, and-- but many people was local people who know the, the whole area.
I didn't know the area at all, but they know the area.
They know the villages, where they are.
They know where to go in the night to, to take potatoes from the-- from the peasant.
And so this was the life.
In the night, they would take-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they go to the-- to the villages, to the-- and take some potatoes.
And this was, was the life.
And--
Yes.
This was not a partisan.
This was just refugees from the-- from the ghettos--
--from around.
Of course, how many--
No, nothing.
No, we haven't got nothing, absolutely nothing.
No, this was-- this was-- this was-- yeah, this was early autumn.
But it was not cold.
It was pretty warm at this time, in the, the beginning, yes.
It was 1942, yes, 1942.
And, and so we start-- one time, we meet some Russian, Russian soldiers.
We meet some.
And, and they take us.
And we went together, and, and the [NON-ENGLISH] start to plan.
And and they, they-- but it was a very bad situation in the woods because it was different-- a mixture of different groups.
It was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was-- there were Ukrainische group.
And we hear there were Ukrainian groups.
They, they killed the Poles.
They kill Poles and take the Poles, the woman and children Poles, in the churches-- in the little churches in the country.
And they burn them.
Oh, it's terrible what they-- what they do.
They-- so we didn't know who is who.
And it was very-- should be very careful who-- whom you meet.
And so we start to organize the, the partisan, the mostly two Russian-- Russian help a lot, because they, they, they was really nice, nice guys.
So we went with them.
And we start to organize-- after a time, there's more and more groups.
And some are coming from the-- from the-- I suppose from Russia, from-- sent special to organize.
Yes, and there was the-- Anadelin was a, a, a, a officer, a Russian, who organized the group that I been-- Anadelin.
And the whole groups-- there was a lot of groups, and the-- mine-- command was Begma, General [NON-ENGLISH] Begma.
A Russian, yes.
He was the mine command.
And after his command, the Russians had start to organize the whole groups.
And we start to go on the line, on the railway line to, to, to mine the, the line-- the line.
To blow them up.
And, yes, it was very, very successful.
We, we went every night somewhere and put some mines under the rail.
And, and it was-- it was-- it was already winter, was, was snow.
And we take the white, white-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call-- white sheets.
And from the woods, we would go to the highway.
We put the mines under the, the, the, the, the-- is coming, the, the--
The train are coming forward with, with food for the army, for the soldiers.
And they-- we blow up the, the locomotives.
And they jump from the carriages and start to shoot in the-- in the woods.
And we shoot them back.
And, and this was the, the, the, the, yes, the, the war.
This was-- this was the part-- I don't know exactly.
But we call this [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means 'woods.' [NON-ENGLISH] must be a, a, a big part of, of, of woods.
It was Pinsk, Sarny-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Pinsk, Sarny, Rowne, and Telekhany.
I remember a few names.
But just I couldn't remember because this is not an area where I lived before or went on--
This was Belorussia, in-between Belorussia and Ukraine.
It was occupied by the Russians.
By the Russians.
When start the, the, the Russian-- German-Russian war is, is the German are going on the Moscow.
Yes, Moscow and Stalingrad was the biggest-- and Leningrad was the biggest fights.
Oh, more non-Jews.
No.
No, I meet only a few Jews.
Yes.
Yes.
This is one way.
But I didn't ask how they escaped, you know-- --you know.
Russian.
Yes--
--mostly Russian.
Oh, yes.
Because I was working for the Russians in [NON-ENGLISH].
I was-- and I was good in Russian, yes.
Yes.
I never think about that.
Yes, we have enough to eat because they have to-- later on, with everything, it was organized.
They have kitchens.
And they have food, where, where they go to the to the farms occupied by the Germans.
And we go at night.
And we take the whole farm with, with, with carriages and horses and everything.
We take in the woods.
We have a lot.
So it's not-- was not a problem.
No.
Were there any women among the partisans?
Yes, yes.
I, I meet one Yiddish woman.
The name is, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Solomin, yeah.
No, no, no.
She was Jewish.
But she's from Pinsk, I suppose, yeah--
--Solomin.
A Pinsk, Pinsk woman, yeah, Solomin.
No.
I don't know.
No.
And, and how long did you stay with the partisans?
Until the-- until the, the Russian come over, until '44.
I went-- one, one time, I, I went away because I was afraid they'd want to take the, the, the rifle from me.
I didn't know.
I was suspicious, very suspicious.
And when we go-- we went some in a-- in a village.
And they start to talk to, to himself without me.
And they took me outside.
And I was very suspicious.
So I run away.
I run away.
I run away, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I go to the village.
And I take a peasant with a horse and a carriage and, and told him to, to take me to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the-- to the canal, to the-- to the canal, yes, to the canal.

I have-- I have-- I have a-- not a friend, a, a, a, a, a, a pair of teachers who was living not far from the-- from, from Janow Poleski, not far, in a-- in a small, country village, a very small country village.
They were teachers in the school.
And they were-- they were from the same little town where my-- where my wife come from.
And we, we all were thinking, maybe we can hide in their place.
But I came back to them, and I was with the rifle.
And they were very afraid.
Because they didn't-- they was afraid that somebody saw me with the rifle in their place.
It could be suspicious.
But I went in the night.
And I told him what's happened.
And I have a rifle with me.
And they keep me on the-- again, in the loft in the house.
And they give me some food.
And I have some clothes in their place because when we was ready to run-- so we put some clothes to their place.
And I-- and then I have some shoes, because my shoes-- what I was walking was destroyed.
And I have some shoes in their place.
And I take-- this is what I went, for clothes and shoes.
And I take my shoes, what I have some shoes what I used to go skiing.
And I-- and I take the shoes.
And they give me some food.
And, and, and, and, and, and next, next night, I went away.
I went away to, to a group of Jews when I know where they are.
But they was not in the partisan, just a group living in the-- in the-- in the woods.
It was a Jewish family.
Reznik-- it was a, a, a, a, a man and, and, and his mother and two sisters.
They were living in-- because they, they, they know the places.
They are in the-- in the timber business.
And they know the places.
And--
They came--
--they were living there.
They came from that area.
They were born in that area, yes.
Yes.
I, I meet some later on in [NON-ENGLISH].
After the war, they went to America.
Oh, not long, but I went again to the-- to the Russians, what I know.
I can trust them.
And I went there and start again in the partisans.
I was--
--with the partisans--
Yeah.
Till the war finished, yeah.
Yes.
Were there any other incidents that you want to talk about?
Not special, no.
I don't remember something special, no.
No.
No.
No.
Oh, yes, there's one more incident what I remember.
[LAUGHS] But it's-- and now it's interesting to, to tell about them, because when you are in such a circumstances, there are incident.
Every, every time is a different when we go.
Oh, I was-- and one time, we went to a mill.
It was a mill what they--
A flour mill.
We want to take some mill.
And the mill was close to the-- to the river.
And we went from the woods to the river.
And on the river was a German boat.
And the German was-- they, they, they, they notice us.
But they, they, they lie down in the-- in the woods, in the other side.
And they let us go through a-- through a open place to the mill.
And when we got back, they start to shoot on us.
Oh, it was-- oh, I, I-- this time I was thinking, it's finished.
[CRYING] And I have a grenade.
And I was keeping-- [LAUGHS] under the-- to myself.
A grenade.
And, and I was thinking, when come to take me, I, I pull the grenade.
[CRYING] But when they shot, they was-- they were afraid, too, because they have some in the woods, too-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] partisans.
So anyways, they shot-- they shot.
And I have a, a, a, a, a, a, a bag under my-- a bag under my [POINTS] spine, a bag.
And when I lie down-- and I put the bag-- I put from the [INAUDIBLE], from-- I put in a higher-- in a higher place.
And they shoot.
And then-- and I was going to the woods.
And they were shooting on the bag.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, I got back safely.
And I was-- that this was-- I was ready to-- I was thinking, this is the finish.
Yes.
The end of the war is-- been in Rowne.
Rowne was the, the town, not, not a-- not a small town.
And, and when the German leave Rowne, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they run away, Rowne was not, not destroyed at all, and-- when we come to the Rowne.
And after a few days, or two days or something, they start to bombard, bombard the city.
And they bombard this--
Germans.
Yes.
Yeah.
And I was, in this time, in the city.
And later on, we run from the city.
And we-- and we went to, to, to, to, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to Russia, yes.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
With the partisan, yes.
Because the headquarters was in [NON-ENGLISH].
The headquarters from the-- Begma.
He, he, he was the, the-- [SIGHS] I don't know what you call it.
The commander, yes, he was.
Why-- one, one episode, I, I-- which is very, for me, important, the [NON-ENGLISH] in the-- in the-- in the ghetto, in [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH], who was a gardener in my-- in, in one of, of my farms.
And he, he keep us on his loft for a night.
And he was the burgermeister of the town because he was a German-- that the German are coming, so they find the German.
So they make him the burgermeister.
And this is-- this is [? without-- ?] yes.
And, and we went-- we have one night when the German was dancing and, and, and drinking in his place.
And, and we were-- we went out there.
I and--
--my wife.
Still living in the ghetto, yes.
Because you, you, you expect every night it's happen.
Maybe not this night, next night.
And a few nights, we, we, we been, after the ghetto.
And when it's happened, this was it.
And then-- yes.
Who?
Yes, yes.
He was a communist, yes.
Yes, he was a very big-- he was General [NON-ENGLISH] Begma, yes.
And we went to his headquarters because he, he has a duty to give some work to everybody who comes.
He runs the office and everything.
So I was sent-- from, from there, I was sent to, to, to [NON-ENGLISH].
It's from Kiev-- is it 20 or 25 kilometers?
[NON-ENGLISH]-- the supplies were in [NON-ENGLISH].
Built a [NON-ENGLISH] for himself.
Yes, very nice.
It was a, a, a monastery once.
And he built a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was close to the [NON-ENGLISH] River.
And, and I was sent there.
Two, two years because I, I-- he, he wants a specialist.
He was looking for a specialist from my nationalities.
I, I remember there was the-- [NON-ENGLISH] was working-- different nationalities working on different lines of-- some are making the, the floors, and the [INAUDIBLE], some different--
My job was the garden.
Yes.
Yes.
So did you meet Khruschev?
Oh, yes.
I meet him twice.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, he talked to me.
But he was not the, the-- in this time, he was only in the [NON-ENGLISH].
He was the secretary of Ukraine, not in the secretary of Russia.
Later on, he was in Russia.
Yeah, here I talk with him, yes.
He asked about, how was it in the partisans?
And this.
Yes.
And I have a vodka with him, too.
[LAUGHTER]
Yes.
And did you have other jobs there, in, in Kiev, after the war?
Oh, yes.
After, after I went [BACKROUND NOISE] away from there because it was not a good place.
It was very isolated.
And so I went away.
And I-- and I find the-- a, a, a person, was Marchenko.
Marchenko, he was in the partisans, too.
And he was the head of [NON-ENGLISH].
So I went to him, and he, he gave me a job.
He gave me a job, [NON-ENGLISH] means to, to, to, to-- oh boy, I have to look to bring what they need for the-- for the shops, for the-- to bring some machinery or else in, in this.
And I have to find-- for them, it's-- you have to find.
This is you.
And what I have to do, I have to-- from the, the army, from the-- from the tankers, I buy [LAUGHS] oil from the tanks.
That's right.
They threw away barrels of oil from, from from, from the army when they, they come.
Yes.
And, and, and I have a-- it's a good job, because I have the possibility to, to, to travel a lot.
Because this is the, the railway.
And when I find out my brother is in [NON-ENGLISH]-- the accident.
Because I've been in the-- in the-- in, in Kiev.
And they start to organize the Polish, Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the people who run.
Refugees.
They're all refugees, Polish and Jewish.
They start to organize.
They call this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- the Polish patriots to go back to Poland.
Polish--
Polish and Jewish.
Jewish people.
A special organization, yes.
And [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] they call this.
I have-- and they-- and they-- it must be somewhere.
No, I haven't got.
And, and when I was there, it was a, a, a, a, a lady, a girl, a woman, was coming from [NON-ENGLISH].
And she says, he, he knows the same name in [NON-ENGLISH].
And [NON-ENGLISH] [SOBBING] is 3,000 kilometers from, from, from Kiev.
And yes, I find out this is my brother.
[CRYING]
